Chapter 8: First-Order Logic — Exercises

Chapter 8: First-Order Logic¶

Russell & Norvig, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (4th ed.), end-of-chapter Exercise prompts — full text is included on this site (same material as the exercises/ch08/ sources in the aima monorepo).

  • Back to Assignments

  • All textbook exercise chapters

Exercise 8.1¶

A logical knowledge base represents the world using a set of sentences with no explicit structure. An analogical representation, on the other hand, has physical structure that corresponds directly to the structure of the thing represented. Consider a road map of your country as an analogical representation of facts about the country—it represents facts with a map language. The two-dimensional structure of the map corresponds to the two-dimensional surface of the area.

  1. Give five examples of symbols in the map language.

  2. An explicit sentence is a sentence that the creator of the representation actually writes down. An implicit sentence is a sentence that results from explicit sentences because of properties of the analogical representation. Give three examples each of implicit and explicit sentences in the map language.

  3. Give three examples of facts about the physical structure of your country that cannot be represented in the map language.

  4. Give two examples of facts that are much easier to express in the map language than in first-order logic.

  5. Give two other examples of useful analogical representations. What are the advantages and disadvantages of each of these languages?

Exercise 8.2¶

Consider a knowledge base containing just two sentences: P(a)P(a)P(a) and P(b)P(b)P(b). Does this knowledge base entail ∀ x P(x)\forall\,x\ P(x)∀x P(x)? Explain your answer in terms of models.

Exercise 8.3¶

Is the sentence ∃ x,y    x = y{\exists\,x,y\;\;} x{{\,=\,}}y∃x,yx=y valid? Explain.

Exercise 8.4¶

Write down a logical sentence such that every world in which it is true contains exactly one object.

Exercise 8.5¶

Write down a logical sentence such that every world in which it is true contains exactly two objects.

Exercise 8.6¶

Consider a symbol vocabulary that contains ccc constant symbols, pkp_kpk​ predicate symbols of each arity kkk, and fkf_kfk​ function symbols of each arity kkk, where 1≤k≤A1\leq k\leq A1≤k≤A. Let the domain size be fixed at DDD. For any given model, each predicate or function symbol is mapped onto a relation or function, respectively, of the same arity. You may assume that the functions in the model allow some input tuples to have no value for the function (i.e., the value is the invisible object). Derive a formula for the number of possible models for a domain with DDD elements. Don’t worry about eliminating redundant combinations.

Exercise 8.7¶

Which of the following are valid (necessarily true) sentences?

  1. (∃x x = x)   ⇒   (∀ y    ∃z y = z)(\exists x\ x{{\,=\,}}x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}({\forall\,y\;\;} \exists z\ y{{\,=\,}}z)(∃x x=x)⇒(∀y∃z y=z).

  2. ∀ x    P(x)∨¬P(x){\forall\,x\;\;} P(x) \lor \lnot P(x)∀xP(x)∨¬P(x).

  3. ∀ x    Smart(x)∨(x = x){\forall\,x\;\;} {Smart}(x) \lor (x{{\,=\,}}x)∀xSmart(x)∨(x=x).

Exercise 8.8¶

Consider a version of the semantics for first-order logic in which models with empty domains are allowed. Give at least two examples of sentences that are valid according to the standard semantics but not according to the new semantics. Discuss which outcome makes more intuitive sense for your examples.

Exercise 8.9¶

Does the fact ¬Spouse(George,Laura)\lnot {Spouse}({George},{Laura})¬Spouse(George,Laura) follow from the facts Jim≠George{Jim}\neq {George}Jim=George and Spouse(Jim,Laura){Spouse}({Jim},{Laura})Spouse(Jim,Laura)? If so, give a proof; if not, supply additional axioms as needed. What happens if we use Spouse{Spouse}Spouse as a unary function symbol instead of a binary predicate?

Exercise 8.10¶

This exercise uses the function MapColor{MapColor}MapColor and predicates In(x,y){In}(x,y)In(x,y), Borders(x,y){Borders}(x,y)Borders(x,y), and Country(x){Country}(x)Country(x), whose arguments are geographical regions, along with constant symbols for various regions. In each of the following we give an English sentence and a number of candidate logical expressions. For each of the logical expressions, state whether it (1) correctly expresses the English sentence; (2) is syntactically invalid and therefore meaningless; or (3) is syntactically valid but does not express the meaning of the English sentence.

  1. Paris and Marseilles are both in France.

    1. In(Paris∧Marseilles,France){In}({Paris} \land {Marseilles}, {France})In(Paris∧Marseilles,France).

    2. In(Paris,France)∧In(Marseilles,France){In}({Paris},{France}) \land {In}({Marseilles},{France})In(Paris,France)∧In(Marseilles,France).

    3. In(Paris,France)∨In(Marseilles,France){In}({Paris},{France}) \lor {In}({Marseilles},{France})In(Paris,France)∨In(Marseilles,France).

  2. There is a country that borders both Iraq and Pakistan.

    1. ∃ c    {\exists\,c\;\;}∃c Country(c)∧Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan){Country}(c) \land {Border}(c,{Iraq}) \land {Border}(c,{Pakistan})Country(c)∧Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan).

    2. ∃ c    {\exists\,c\;\;}∃c Country(c)   ⇒   [Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan)]{Country}(c) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}[{Border}(c,{Iraq}) \land {Border}(c,{Pakistan})]Country(c)⇒[Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan)].

    3. [∃ c    [{\exists\,c\;\;}[∃c Country(c)]   ⇒   [Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan)]{Country}(c)] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}[{Border}(c,{Iraq}) \land {Border}(c,{Pakistan})]Country(c)]⇒[Border(c,Iraq)∧Border(c,Pakistan)].

    4. ∃ c    {\exists\,c\;\;}∃c Border(Country(c),Iraq∧Pakistan){Border}({Country}(c),{Iraq} \land {Pakistan})Border(Country(c),Iraq∧Pakistan).

  3. All countries that border Ecuador are in South America.

    1. ∀ c    Country(c)∧Border(c,Ecuador)   ⇒   In(c,SouthAmerica){\forall\,c\;\;} Country(c) \land {Border}(c,{Ecuador}) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{In}(c,{SouthAmerica})∀cCountry(c)∧Border(c,Ecuador)⇒In(c,SouthAmerica).

    2. ∀ c    Country(c)   ⇒   [Border(c,Ecuador)   ⇒   In(c,SouthAmerica)]{\forall\,c\;\;} {Country}(c) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}[{Border}(c,{Ecuador}) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{In}(c,{SouthAmerica})]∀cCountry(c)⇒[Border(c,Ecuador)⇒In(c,SouthAmerica)].

    3. ∀ c    [Country(c)   ⇒   Border(c,Ecuador)]   ⇒   In(c,SouthAmerica){\forall\,c\;\;} [{Country}(c) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Border}(c,{Ecuador})] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{In}(c,{SouthAmerica})∀c[Country(c)⇒Border(c,Ecuador)]⇒In(c,SouthAmerica).

    4. ∀ c    Country(c)∧Border(c,Ecuador)∧In(c,SouthAmerica){\forall\,c\;\;} Country(c) \land {Border}(c,{Ecuador}) \land {In}(c,{SouthAmerica})∀cCountry(c)∧Border(c,Ecuador)∧In(c,SouthAmerica).

  4. No region in South America borders any region in Europe.

    1. ¬[∃ c,d    In(c,SouthAmerica)∧In(d,Europe)∧Borders(c,d)]\lnot [{\exists\,c,d\;\;} {In}(c,{SouthAmerica}) \land {In}(d,{Europe}) \land {Borders}(c,d)]¬[∃c,dIn(c,SouthAmerica)∧In(d,Europe)∧Borders(c,d)].

    2. ∀ c,d    [In(c,SouthAmerica)∧In(d,Europe)]   ⇒   ¬Borders(c,d)]{\forall\,c,d\;\;} [{In}(c,{SouthAmerica}) \land {In}(d,{Europe})] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}\lnot {Borders}(c,d)]∀c,d[In(c,SouthAmerica)∧In(d,Europe)]⇒¬Borders(c,d)].

    3. ¬∀ c    In(c,SouthAmerica)   ⇒   ∃ d    In(d,Europe)∧<br>¬Borders(c,d)\lnot {\forall\,c\;\;} {In}(c,{SouthAmerica}) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{\exists\,d\;\;} {In}(d,{Europe}) \land<br> \lnot {Borders}(c,d)¬∀cIn(c,SouthAmerica)⇒∃dIn(d,Europe)∧<br>¬Borders(c,d).

    4. ∀ c    In(c,SouthAmerica)   ⇒   ∀ d    In(d,Europe)   ⇒   ¬Borders(c,d){\forall\,c\;\;} {In}(c,{SouthAmerica}) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{\forall\,d\;\;} {In}(d,{Europe}) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}\lnot {Borders}(c,d)∀cIn(c,SouthAmerica)⇒∀dIn(d,Europe)⇒¬Borders(c,d).

  5. No two adjacent countries have the same map color.

    1. ∀ x,y    ¬Country(x)∨¬Country(y)∨¬Borders(x,y)∨{\forall\,x,y\;\;} \lnot {Country}(x) \lor \lnot {Country}(y) \lor \lnot {Borders}(x,y) \lor {}∀x,y¬Country(x)∨¬Country(y)∨¬Borders(x,y)∨
      ¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y))\lnot ({MapColor}(x) = {MapColor}(y))¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y)).

    2. ∀ x,y    (Country(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y)∧¬(x=y))   ⇒   {\forall\,x,y\;\;} ({Country}(x) \land {Country}(y) \land {Borders}(x,y) \land \lnot(x=y)) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{}∀x,y(Country(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y)∧¬(x=y))⇒
      ¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y))\lnot ({MapColor}(x) = {MapColor}(y))¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y)).

    3. ∀ x,y    Country(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y)∧{\forall\,x,y\;\;} {Country}(x) \land {Country}(y) \land {Borders}(x,y) \land {}∀x,yCountry(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y)∧
      ¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y))\lnot ({MapColor}(x) = {MapColor}(y))¬(MapColor(x)=MapColor(y)).

    4. ∀ x,y    (Country(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y))   ⇒   MapColor(x≠y){\forall\,x,y\;\;} ({Country}(x) \land {Country}(y) \land {Borders}(x,y) ) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{MapColor}(x\neq y)∀x,y(Country(x)∧Country(y)∧Borders(x,y))⇒MapColor(x=y).

Exercise 8.11¶

Consider a vocabulary with the following symbols:

Occupation(p,o){Occupation}(p,o)Occupation(p,o): Predicate. Person ppp has occupation ooo.

Customer(p1,p2){Customer}(p1,p2)Customer(p1,p2): Predicate. Person p1p1p1 is a customer of person p2p2p2.

Boss(p1,p2){Boss}(p1,p2)Boss(p1,p2): Predicate. Person p1p1p1 is a boss of person p2p2p2.

Doctor{Doctor}Doctor, Surgeon {Surgeon}Surgeon, Lawyer {Lawyer}Lawyer, Actor {Actor}Actor: Constants denoting occupations.

Emily{Emily}Emily, Joe {Joe}Joe: Constants denoting people.

Use these symbols to write the following assertions in first-order logic:

  1. Emily is either a surgeon or a lawyer.

  2. Joe is an actor, but he also holds another job.

  3. All surgeons are doctors.

  4. Joe does not have a lawyer (i.e., is not a customer of any lawyer).

  5. Emily has a boss who is a lawyer.

  6. There exists a lawyer all of whose customers are doctors.

  7. Every surgeon has a lawyer.

Exercise 8.12¶

In each of the following we give an English sentence and a number of candidate logical expressions. For each of the logical expressions, state whether it (1) correctly expresses the English sentence; (2) is syntactically invalid and therefore meaningless; or (3) is syntactically valid but does not express the meaning of the English sentence.

  1. Every cat loves its mother or father.

    1. ∀ x    Cat(x)   ⇒   Loves(x,Mother(x)∨Father(x)){\forall\,x\;\;} {Cat}(x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Loves}(x,{Mother}(x)\lor {Father}(x))∀xCat(x)⇒Loves(x,Mother(x)∨Father(x)).

    2. ∀ x    ¬Cat(x)∨Loves(x,Mother(x))∨Loves(x,Father(x)){\forall\,x\;\;} \lnot {Cat}(x) \lor {Loves}(x,{Mother}(x)) \lor {Loves}(x,{Father}(x))∀x¬Cat(x)∨Loves(x,Mother(x))∨Loves(x,Father(x)).

    3. ∀ x    Cat(x)∧(Loves(x,Mother(x))∨Loves(x,Father(x))){\forall\,x\;\;} {Cat}(x) \land ({Loves}(x,{Mother}(x))\lor {Loves}(x,{Father}(x)))∀xCat(x)∧(Loves(x,Mother(x))∨Loves(x,Father(x))).

  2. Every dog who loves one of its brothers is happy.

    1. ∀ x    Dog(x)∧(∃y Brother(y,x)∧Loves(x,y))   ⇒   Happy(x){\forall\,x\;\;} {Dog}(x) \land (\exists y\ {Brother}(y,x) \land {Loves}(x,y)) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Happy}(x)∀xDog(x)∧(∃y Brother(y,x)∧Loves(x,y))⇒Happy(x).

    2. ∀ x,y    Dog(x)∧Brother(y,x)∧Loves(x,y)   ⇒   Happy(x){\forall\,x,y\;\;} {Dog}(x) \land {Brother}(y,x) \land {Loves}(x,y) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Happy}(x)∀x,yDog(x)∧Brother(y,x)∧Loves(x,y)⇒Happy(x).

    3. ∀ x    Dog(x)∧[∀ y    Brother(y,x)    ⇔    Loves(x,y)]   ⇒   Happy(x){\forall\,x\;\;} {Dog}(x) \land [{\forall\,y\;\;} {Brother}(y,x) {\;\;{\Leftrightarrow}\;\;}{Loves}(x,y)] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Happy}(x)∀xDog(x)∧[∀yBrother(y,x)⇔Loves(x,y)]⇒Happy(x).

  3. No dog bites a child of its owner.

    1. ∀ x    Dog(x)   ⇒   ¬Bites(x,Child(Owner(x))){\forall\,x\;\;} {Dog}(x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}\lnot {Bites}(x,{Child}({Owner}(x)))∀xDog(x)⇒¬Bites(x,Child(Owner(x))).

    2. ¬∃ x,y    Dog(x)∧Child(y,Owner(x))∧Bites(x,y)\lnot {\exists\,x,y\;\;} {Dog}(x) \land {Child}(y,{Owner}(x)) \land {Bites}(x,y)¬∃x,yDog(x)∧Child(y,Owner(x))∧Bites(x,y).

    3. ∀ x    Dog(x)   ⇒   (∀ y    Child(y,Owner(x))   ⇒   ¬Bites(x,y)){\forall\,x\;\;} {Dog}(x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}({\forall\,y\;\;} {Child}(y,{Owner}(x)) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}\lnot {Bites}(x,y))∀xDog(x)⇒(∀yChild(y,Owner(x))⇒¬Bites(x,y)).

    4. ¬∃ x    Dog(x)   ⇒   (∃ y    Child(y,Owner(x))∧Bites(x,y))\lnot {\exists\,x\;\;} {Dog}(x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}({\exists\,y\;\;} {Child}(y,{Owner}(x)) \land {Bites}(x,y))¬∃xDog(x)⇒(∃yChild(y,Owner(x))∧Bites(x,y)).

  4. Everyone’s zip code within a state has the same first digit.

    1. ∀ x,s,z1    [State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧Zip(x) = z1]   ⇒   {\forall\,x,s,z_1\;\;} [{State}(s) \land {LivesIn}(x,s) \land {Zip}(x){{\,=\,}}z_1] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{}∀x,s,z1​[State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧Zip(x)=z1​]⇒
      [∀ y,z2    LivesIn(y,s)∧Zip(y) = z2   ⇒   Digit(1,z1) = Digit(1,z2)][{\forall\,y,z_2\;\;} {LivesIn}(y,s) \land {Zip}(y){{\,=\,}}z_2 {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Digit}(1,z_1) {{\,=\,}}{Digit}(1,z_2) ][∀y,z2​LivesIn(y,s)∧Zip(y)=z2​⇒Digit(1,z1​)=Digit(1,z2​)].

    2. ∀ x,s    [State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧∃ z1    Zip(x) = z1]   ⇒   {\forall\,x,s\;\;} [{State}(s) \land {LivesIn}(x,s) \land {\exists\,z_1\;\;} {Zip}(x){{\,=\,}}z_1] {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{}∀x,s[State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧∃z1​Zip(x)=z1​]⇒
      [∀ y,z2    LivesIn(y,s)∧Zip(y) = z2∧Digit(1,z1) = Digit(1,z2)] [{\forall\,y,z_2\;\;} {LivesIn}(y,s) \land {Zip}(y){{\,=\,}}z_2 \land {Digit}(1,z_1) {{\,=\,}}{Digit}(1,z_2) ][∀y,z2​LivesIn(y,s)∧Zip(y)=z2​∧Digit(1,z1​)=Digit(1,z2​)].

    3. ∀ x,y,s    State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧LivesIn(y,s)   ⇒   Digit(1,Zip(x) = Zip(y)){\forall\,x,y,s\;\;} {State}(s) \land {LivesIn}(x,s) \land {LivesIn}(y,s) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{Digit}(1,{Zip}(x){{\,=\,}}{Zip}(y))∀x,y,sState(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧LivesIn(y,s)⇒Digit(1,Zip(x)=Zip(y)).

    4. ∀ x,y,s    State(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧LivesIn(y,s)   ⇒   {\forall\,x,y,s\;\;} {State}(s) \land {LivesIn}(x,s) \land {LivesIn}(y,s) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{}∀x,y,sState(s)∧LivesIn(x,s)∧LivesIn(y,s)⇒
      Digit(1,Zip(x)) = Digit(1,Zip(y)){Digit}(1,{Zip}(x)) {{\,=\,}}{Digit}(1,{Zip}(y))Digit(1,Zip(x))=Digit(1,Zip(y)).

Exercise 8.13¶

Complete the following exercises about logical sentences:

  1. Translate into good, natural English (no xxxs or yyys!):

∀ x,y,l    SpeaksLanguage(x,l)∧SpeaksLanguage(y,l)  ⟹  Understands(x,y)∧Understands(y,x).<br>{\forall\,x,y,l\;\;} SpeaksLanguage(x, l) \land SpeaksLanguage(y, l) \implies Understands(x, y) \land Understands(y,x).<br>∀x,y,lSpeaksLanguage(x,l)∧SpeaksLanguage(y,l)⟹Understands(x,y)∧Understands(y,x).<br>
(1)

  1. Explain why this sentence is entailed by the sentence

∀ x,y,l    SpeaksLanguage(x,l)∧SpeaksLanguage(y,l)  ⟹  Understands(x,y).<br>{\forall\,x,y,l\;\;} SpeaksLanguage(x, l) \land SpeaksLanguage(y, l) \implies Understands(x, y).<br>∀x,y,lSpeaksLanguage(x,l)∧SpeaksLanguage(y,l)⟹Understands(x,y).<br>
(2)

  1. Translate into first-order logic the following sentences:

    1. Understanding leads to friendship.

    2. Friendship is transitive.

    Remember to define all predicates, functions, and constants you use.

Exercise 8.14¶

True or false? Explain.

  1. ∃ x    x = Rumpelstiltskin{\exists\,x\;\;} x{{\,=\,}}{Rumpelstiltskin}∃xx=Rumpelstiltskin is a valid (necessarily true) sentence of first-order logic.

  2. Every existentially quantified sentence in first-order logic is true in any model that contains exactly one object.

  3. ∀ x,y    x = y{\forall\,x,y\;\;} x{{\,=\,}}y∀x,yx=yis satisfiable.

Exercise 8.15¶

Rewrite the first two Peano axioms in Section Peano-section as a single axiom that defines NatNum(x){NatNum}(x)NatNum(x) so as to exclude the possibility of natural numbers except for those generated by the successor function.

Exercise 8.16¶

Equation (pit-biconditional-equation) on page pit-biconditional-equation defines the conditions under which a square is breezy. Here we consider two other ways to describe this aspect of the wumpus world.

  1. We can write [diagnostic rule] leading from observed effects to hidden causes. For finding pits, the obvious diagnostic rules say that if a square is breezy, some adjacent square must contain a pit; and if a square is not breezy, then no adjacent square contains a pit. Write these two rules in first-order logic and show that their conjunction is logically equivalent to Equation (pit-biconditional-equation).

  2. We can write [causal rule] leading from cause to effect. One obvious causal rule is that a pit causes all adjacent squares to be breezy. Write this rule in first-order logic, explain why it is incomplete compared to Equation (pit-biconditional-equation), and supply the missing axiom.

Exercise 8.17¶

Write axioms describing the predicates Grandchild{Grandchild}Grandchild, Greatgrandparent{Greatgrandparent}Greatgrandparent, Ancestor{Ancestor}Ancestor, Brother{Brother}Brother, Sister{Sister}Sister, Daughter{Daughter}Daughter, Son{Son}Son, FirstCousin{FirstCousin}FirstCousin, BrotherInLaw{BrotherInLaw}BrotherInLaw, SisterInLaw{SisterInLaw}SisterInLaw, Aunt{Aunt}Aunt, and Uncle{Uncle}Uncle. Find out the proper definition of mmmth cousin nnn times removed, and write the definition in first-order logic. Now write down the basic facts depicted in the family tree in Figure family1-figure. Using a suitable logical reasoning system, it all the sentences you have written down, and it who are Elizabeth’s grandchildren, Diana’s brothers-in-law, Zara’s great-grandparents, and Eugenie’s ancestors.

Exercise 8.18¶

Write down a sentence asserting that + is a commutative function. Does your sentence follow from the Peano axioms? If so, explain why; if not, give a model in which the axioms are true and your sentence is false.

Exercise 8.19¶

Explain what is wrong with the following proposed definition of the set membership predicate

∀,x,s;;x∈x∣s{\forall,x,s;;} x \in {x|s}∀,x,s;;x∈x∣s
(3)

∀,x,s;;x∈s  ⟹  ∀,y;;x∈y∣s{\forall,x,s;;} x \in s \implies {\forall,y;;} x \in {y|s}∀,x,s;;x∈s⟹∀,y;;x∈y∣s
(4)

Exercise 8.20¶

Using the set axioms as examples, write axioms for the list domain, including all the constants, functions, and predicates mentioned in the chapter.

Exercise 8.21¶

Explain what is wrong with the following proposed definition of adjacent squares in the wumpus world:

∀ x,y    Adjacent([x,y],[x+1,y])∧Adjacent([x,y],[x,y+1]) .{\forall\,x,y\;\;} {Adjacent}([x,y], [x+1, y]) \land {Adjacent}([x,y], [x, y+1])\ .∀x,yAdjacent([x,y],[x+1,y])∧Adjacent([x,y],[x,y+1]) .
(5)

Exercise 8.22¶

Write out the axioms required for reasoning about the wumpus’s location, using a constant symbol Wumpus{Wumpus}Wumpus and a binary predicate At(Wumpus,Location){At}({Wumpus}, {Location})At(Wumpus,Location). Remember that there is only one wumpus.

Exercise 8.23¶

Assuming predicates Parent(p,q){Parent}(p,q)Parent(p,q) and Female(p){Female}(p)Female(p) and constants Joan{Joan}Joan and Kevin{Kevin}Kevin, with the obvious meanings, express each of the following sentences in first-order logic. (You may use the abbreviation ∃1\exists^{1}∃1 to mean “there exists exactly one.”)

  1. Joan has a daughter (possibly more than one, and possibly sons as well).

  2. Joan has exactly one daughter (but may have sons as well).

  3. Joan has exactly one child, a daughter.

  4. Joan and Kevin have exactly one child together.

  5. Joan has at least one child with Kevin, and no children with anyone else.

Exercise 8.24¶

Arithmetic assertions can be written in first-order logic with the predicate symbol <<<, the function symbols +{+}+ and ×{\times}×, and the constant symbols 0 and 1. Additional predicates can also be defined with biconditionals.

  1. Represent the property “xxx is an even number.”

  2. Represent the property “xxx is prime.”

  3. Goldbach’s conjecture is the conjecture (unproven as yet) that every even number is equal to the sum of two primes. Represent this conjecture as a logical sentence.

Exercise 8.25¶

In Chapter csp-chapter, we used equality to indicate the relation between a variable and its value. For instance, we wrote WA = red{WA}{{\,=\,}}{red}WA=red to mean that Western Australia is colored red. Representing this in first-order logic, we must write more verbosely ColorOf(WA) = red{ColorOf}({WA}){{\,=\,}}{red}ColorOf(WA)=red. What incorrect inference could be drawn if we wrote sentences such as WA = red{WA}{{\,=\,}}{red}WA=red directly as logical assertions?

Exercise 8.26¶

Write in first-order logic the assertion that every key and at least one of every pair of socks will eventually be lost forever, using only the following vocabulary: Key(x){Key}(x)Key(x), xxx is a key; Sock(x){Sock}(x)Sock(x), xxx is a sock; Pair(x,y){Pair}(x,y)Pair(x,y), xxx and yyy are a pair; Now{Now}Now, the current time; Before(t1,t2){Before}(t_1,t_2)Before(t1​,t2​), time t1t_1t1​ comes before time t2t_2t2​; Lost(x,t){Lost}(x,t)Lost(x,t), object xxx is lost at time ttt.

Exercise 8.27¶

For each of the following sentences in English, decide if the accompanying first-order logic sentence is a good translation. If not, explain why not and correct it. (Some sentences may have more than one error!)

  1. No two people have the same social security number.

    ¬∃ x,y,n    Person(x)∧Person(y)   ⇒   [HasSS#(x,n)∧HasSS#(y,n)].\lnot {\exists\,x,y,n\;\;} {Person}(x) \land {Person}(y) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}[{HasSS}\#(x,n) \land {HasSS}\#(y,n)].¬∃x,y,nPerson(x)∧Person(y)⇒[HasSS#(x,n)∧HasSS#(y,n)].
    (6)


  2. John’s social security number is the same as Mary’s.

    ∃ n    HasSS#(John,n)∧HasSS#(Mary,n).{\exists\,n\;\;} {HasSS}\#({John},n) \land {HasSS}\#({Mary},n).∃nHasSS#(John,n)∧HasSS#(Mary,n).
    (7)


  3. Everyone’s social security number has nine digits.

    ∀ x,n    Person(x)   ⇒   [HasSS#(x,n)∧Digits(n,9)].{\forall\,x,n\;\;} {Person}(x) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}[{HasSS}\#(x,n) \land {Digits}(n,9)].∀x,nPerson(x)⇒[HasSS#(x,n)∧Digits(n,9)].
    (8)


  4. Rewrite each of the above (uncorrected) sentences using a function symbol SS#{SS}\#SS# instead of the predicate HasSS#{HasSS}\#HasSS#.

Exercise 8.28¶

Translate into first-order logic the sentence “Everyone’s DNA is unique and is derived from their parents’ DNA.” You must specify the precise intended meaning of your vocabulary terms. (Hint: Do not use the predicate Unique(x){Unique}(x)Unique(x), since uniqueness is not really a property of an object in itself!)

Exercise 8.29¶

For each of the following sentences in English, decide if the accompanying first-order logic sentence is a good translation. If not, explain why not and correct it.

  1. Any apartment in London has lower rent than some apartments in Paris.

∀x[Apt(x)∧In(x,London)]  ⟹  ∃y([Apt(y)∧In(y,Paris)]  ⟹  (Rent(x)<Rent(y)))\forall {x} [{Apt}(x) \land {In}(x,{London})] \implies \exists {y} ([{Apt}(y) \land {In}(y,{Paris})] \implies ({Rent}(x) < {Rent}(y)))∀x[Apt(x)∧In(x,London)]⟹∃y([Apt(y)∧In(y,Paris)]⟹(Rent(x)<Rent(y)))
(9)

  1. There is exactly one apartment in Paris with rent below $1000.

∃xApt(x)∧In(x,Paris)∧∀y[Apt(y)∧In(y,Paris)∧(Rent(y)<Dollars(1000))]  ⟹  (y=x)\exists {x} {Apt}(x) \land {In}(x,{Paris}) \land \forall{y} [{Apt}(y) \land {In}(y,{Paris}) \land ({Rent}(y) < {Dollars}(1000))] \implies (y = x)∃xApt(x)∧In(x,Paris)∧∀y[Apt(y)∧In(y,Paris)∧(Rent(y)<Dollars(1000))]⟹(y=x)
(10)

  1. If an apartment is more expensive than all apartments in London, it must be in Moscow.

∀xApt(x)∧[∀yApt(y)∧In(y,London)∧(Rent(x)>Rent(y))]  ⟹  In(x,Moscow).\forall{x} {Apt}(x) \land [\forall{y} {Apt}(y) \land {In}(y,{London}) \land ({Rent}(x) > {Rent}(y))] \implies {In}(x,{Moscow}).∀xApt(x)∧[∀yApt(y)∧In(y,London)∧(Rent(x)>Rent(y))]⟹In(x,Moscow).
(11)

Exercise 8.30¶

Represent the following sentences in first-order logic, using a consistent vocabulary (which you must define):

  1. Some students took French in spring 2001.

  2. Every student who takes French passes it.

  3. Only one student took Greek in spring 2001.

  4. The best score in Greek is always higher than the best score in French.

  5. Every person who buys a policy is smart.

  6. No person buys an expensive policy.

  7. There is an agent who sells policies only to people who are not insured.

  8. There is a barber who shaves all men in town who do not shave themselves.

  9. A person born in the UK, each of whose parents is a UK citizen or a UK resident, is a UK citizen by birth.

  10. A person born outside the UK, one of whose parents is a UK citizen by birth, is a UK citizen by descent.

  11. Politicians can fool some of the people all of the time, and they can fool all of the people some of the time, but they can’t fool all of the people all of the time.

  12. All Greeks speak the same language. (Use Speaks(x,l){Speaks}(x,l)Speaks(x,l) to mean that person xxx speaks language lll.)

Exercise 8.31¶

Represent the following sentences in first-order logic, using a consistent vocabulary (which you must define):

  1. Some students took French in spring 2001.

  2. Every student who takes French passes it.

  3. Only one student took Greek in spring 2001.

  4. The best score in Greek is always higher than the best score in French.

  5. Every person who buys a policy is smart.

  6. No person buys an expensive policy.

  7. There is an agent who sells policies only to people who are not insured.

  8. There is a barber who shaves all men in town who do not shave themselves.

  9. A person born in the UK, each of whose parents is a UK citizen or a UK resident, is a UK citizen by birth.

  10. A person born outside the UK, one of whose parents is a UK citizen by birth, is a UK citizen by descent.

  11. Politicians can fool some of the people all of the time, and they can fool all of the people some of the time, but they can’t fool all of the people all of the time.

  12. All Greeks speak the same language. (Use Speaks(x,l){Speaks}(x,l)Speaks(x,l) to mean that person xxx speaks language lll.)

Exercise 8.32¶

Write a general set of facts and axioms to represent the assertion “Wellington heard about Napoleon’s death” and to correctly answer the question “Did Napoleon hear about Wellington’s death?”

Exercise 8.33¶

Extend the vocabulary from Section circuits-section to define addition for nnn-bit binary numbers. Then encode the description of the four-bit adder in Figure 4bit-adder-figure, and pose the queries needed to verify that it is in fact correct.

Exercise 8.34¶

The circuit representation in the chapter is more detailed than necessary if we care only about circuit functionality. A simpler formulation describes any mmm-input, nnn-output gate or circuit using a predicate with m+nm+nm+n arguments, such that the predicate is true exactly when the inputs and outputs are consistent. For example, NOT gates are described by the binary predicate NOT(i,o){NOT}(i,o)NOT(i,o), for which NOT(0,1){NOT}(0,1)NOT(0,1) and NOT(1,0){NOT}(1,0)NOT(1,0) are known. Compositions of gates are defined by conjunctions of gate predicates in which shared variables indicate direct connections. For example, a NAND circuit can be composed from AND{AND}ANDs and NOT{NOT}NOTs:

∀ i1,i2,oa,o    AND(i1,i2,oa)∧NOT(oa,o)   ⇒   NAND(i1,i2,o) .{\forall\,i_1,i_2,o_a,o\;\;} {AND}(i_1,i_2,o_a) \land {NOT}(o_a,o) {\:\;{\Rightarrow}\:\;}{NAND}(i_1,i_2,o)\ .∀i1​,i2​,oa​,oAND(i1​,i2​,oa​)∧NOT(oa​,o)⇒NAND(i1​,i2​,o) .
(12)

Using this representation, define the one-bit adder in Figure adder-figure and the four-bit adder in Figure adder-figure, and explain what queries you would use to verify the designs. What kinds of queries are not supported by this representation that are supported by the representation in Section circuits-section?

Exercise 8.35¶

Obtain a passport application for your country, identify the rules determining eligibility for a passport, and translate them into first-order logic, following the steps outlined in Section circuits-section

Exercise 8.36¶

Consider a first-order logical knowledge base that describes worlds containing people, songs, albums (e.g., “Meet the Beatles”) and disks (i.e., particular physical instances of CDs). The vocabulary contains the following symbols:

CopyOf(d,a){CopyOf}(d,a)CopyOf(d,a): Predicate. Disk ddd is a copy of album aaa.

Owns(p,d){Owns}(p,d)Owns(p,d): Predicate. Person ppp owns disk ddd.

Sings(p,s,a){Sings}(p,s,a)Sings(p,s,a): Album aaa includes a recording of song sss sung by person ppp.

Wrote(p,s){Wrote}(p,s)Wrote(p,s): Person ppp wrote song sss.

McCartney{McCartney}McCartney, Gershwin{Gershwin}Gershwin, BHoliday{BHoliday}BHoliday, Joe{Joe}Joe, EleanorRigby{EleanorRigby}EleanorRigby, TheManILove{TheManILove}TheManILove, Revolver{Revolver}Revolver: Constants with the obvious meanings.

Express the following statements in first-order logic:

  1. Gershwin wrote “The Man I Love.”

  2. Gershwin did not write “Eleanor Rigby.”

  3. Either Gershwin or McCartney wrote “The Man I Love.”

  4. Joe has written at least one song.

  5. Joe owns a copy of Revolver.

  6. Every song that McCartney sings on Revolver was written by McCartney.

  7. Gershwin did not write any of the songs on Revolver.

  8. Every song that Gershwin wrote has been recorded on some album. (Possibly different songs are recorded on different albums.)

  9. There is a single album that contains every song that Joe has written.

  10. Joe owns a copy of an album that has Billie Holiday singing “The Man I Love.”

  11. Joe owns a copy of every album that has a song sung by McCartney. (Of course, each different album is instantiated in a different physical CD.)

  12. Joe owns a copy of every album on which all the songs are sung by Billie Holiday.
